title-group.article-title: Discovery of antimicrobial peptides targeting Acinetobacter baumannii via a pre-trained and fine-tuned few-shot learning-based pipeline

abstract.p: Acinetobacter baumannii, a robust Gram-negative bacterium known for causing nosocomial infections, exhibiting multidrug resistance, and lacking antimicrobial peptides that target it, remains hard to treat. Here, we report a few-shot learning pipeline integrating classification, ranking, and regression modules. Each module is trained via a few-shot learning strategy involving pre-training and multiple fine-tuning steps, incorporating similar and true data for fine-tuning, to identify potent AMPs against Acinetobacter baumannii. This pipeline effectively scans complete libraries of hexapeptides, heptapeptides, and octapeptides (encompassing tens of billions of candidates) despite the extreme scarcity of training data. Results show it discovers AMPs active against Acinetobacter baumannii and Candida albicans, with low off-target toxicity and negligible drug resistance susceptibility. Additionally, EME7(7) controls Acinetobacter baumannii pneumonia in mice without kidney injury, a contrast to the observed effects of polymyxin B. This work provides a paradigm for addressing challenges of limited data availability.

The authors introduce a deep learning pipeline integrating classification, ranking, and regression modules, in which each module is trained via a few-shot learning strategy involving pretraining and multiple fine-tuning steps, to identify potent AMPs against Acinetobacter baumannii.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: The rapid proliferation of infections caused by antibiotic-resistant pathogens has emerged as a significant determinant impacting human health, resulting in a global estimate of 700 thousand annual fatalities1. Furthermore, this issue is projected to escalate dramatically, reaching an alarming toll of 10 million deaths per year by 20502. Among the various pathogens responsible for such infections, Gram-negative bacteria, particularly A. baumannii, pose a substantial threat by inducing severe clinical complications, accounting for approximately 67% of infected patients3. A. baumannii, a nosocomial pathogen, predominantly afflicts critically ill individuals with ventilator-associated infections, consistently exhibiting multidrug resistance due to its resilient outer membrane and the rapid mutability of its adaptive genome in response to adverse conditions and stress4–6. In addition, A. baumannii infection is associated with a range of severe clinical conditions, including ventilator-associated pneumonia, septicemia, sepsis, necrotizing pneumonia, and septic shock. In critical cases, these complications can ultimately lead to patient mortality. Hence, the World Health Organization (WHO) has classified it as the first rank of critical strains7. While polymyxin B serves as the last line of defense against A. baumannii, its use is associated with the risk of kidney injury and the gradual development of drug resistance8–10. The urgent need for new antimicrobial agents has become evident. Unfortunately, the recent decades witness only limited new antibiotics discovery and development11,12. Addressing this critical issue is essential to combat the growing threat of antibiotic-resistant infections and to safeguard public health.

Antimicrobial peptides (AMPs) are a class of small peptides typically composed of 10-50 amino acid residues, exhibiting amphipathic properties and possessing a net positive charge13. Given their membrane lytic killing mechanism, and reduced likelihood of inducing resistance, AMPs have emerged as promising alternatives to conventional antibiotics and potential candidates for next-generation antimicrobial agents14,15. Notably, a considerable number of AMPs have been designed to target pathogens such as P. aeruginosa, E. coli, and S. aureus, with thousands of variants currently available. While dozens of AMPs are currently undergoing evaluation in clinical trials16. However, the discovery of novel AMPs against A. baumannii has proven challenging through conventional screening approaches. The number of AMPs identified for A. baumannii is less than one-tenth compared to those discovered for the above pathogens. Consequently, the development of ideal AMPs specifically targeting A. baumannii with attributes such as low production costs, minimal off-target toxicity, and good solubility, remains a significant hurdle17.

Currently, the advent of machine learning (ML) techniques, including natural language processing, pattern insertion, evolutionary algorithms, binary classifiers, and deep generative models, has provided opportunities to swiftly navigate the extensive sequence space of AMPs through in silico exploration5,18–24. Notably, we have recently developed a sequential model ensemble pipeline (SMEP) that incorporates classification, ranking, and regression modules, resulting in a notable improvement in AMP mining accuracy, reaching 98.2% (54/55), validated by wet-laboratory experiments25,26. Nevertheless, it is important to acknowledge that the efficacy of these powerful ML models relies heavily on the availability of large quantities of high-quality training data (1762 positive AMPs). Unfortunately, the reported data for A. baumannii remains limited, comprising only hundreds of sequences [148 sequences in GRAMPA dataset (dataset 1)], far less than that number of AMPs against other strains. This scarcity of data poses a major challenge in the training of robust ML models tailored specifically to address the characteristics and properties of AMPs against A. baumannii.

ML methods have shown robust accuracies in predicting functions of drugs or materials with a large number of data, but they are likely to yield suboptimal predictions without further tuning or regularization27–30. To address the problem in this study, we introduce a few-shot learning-based sequential ensemble model pipeline (FSLSMEP). Particularly, we propose a pre-training and multiple fine-tuning strategy to tackle the challenge of working with a dataset of only 148 sequences (Fig. 1a). At a macroscopic level, we employed the SMEP funnel to progressively narrow down the search space. At a microscopic level, we selected the unsupervised pre-training model ESM-1b31, which was trained on a vast collection of 250 million protein sequences, owing to its exceptional performance in predicting the biological activity of sequences after fine-tuning32–34. Our approach encompassed two stages of fine-tuning: using a relatively large dataset of AMPs targeting P. aeruginosa first and utilizing a smaller dataset of AMPs specifically against A. baumannii second. By employing this strategy, we observed improved performance in the classification, ranking, and regression models compared to models trained solely on 148 sequences. Consequently, we successfully identified a series of highly potent AMPs from the comprehensive search space encompassing whole hexapeptide, heptapeptide, and octapeptide libraries (Fig. 1c).Fig. 1Overview of the FSLSMEP.
a, Data collected from Grampa dataset (dataset 1), including 1186 anti-P. aeruginosa AMPs (similar data) and 148 anti-A. baumannii AMPs (true data). b, Elaborating on the detailed training procedures of the EME pipeline. ESM served as the pre-trained model choice for classification and regression models. These models were then fine-tuned using similar data encompassing 1186 anti-P. aeruginosa AMPs and the true data of 148 anti-A. baumannii AMPs in sequence (employing peptide sequences as input). The ranking model employed MLP, which was initially trained with anti-P. aeruginosa AMPs and subsequently fine-tuned using anti-A. baumannii AMPs, with physicochemical descriptors as the input. c, The comprehensive FSLSMEP framework consists of empirical selection, classification, ranking, regression modules, and wet-lab experiments. The process involved a sequential reduction of the hexapeptide search space, progressively advancing through the stages of the pipeline. The initial hexapeptide library encompassed 64,000,000 sequences. As each stage unfolded, the candidate pool size was diminished to 3,931,718, 122,673, and eventually 500. The terminal step involved the synthesis of the top-10 peptides predicted by the regression module, which were subsequently subjected to MIC testing via wet-laboratory validation. d, The identified EME AMPs underwent in vitro assays including MIC, cytotoxicity and killing mechanism measurements, and in vivo assays including therapeutic efficacy and toxicity of AMPs. Note: images of mice, cells, lung, kidney, and test tubes in d are created in BioRender. Ji, J. (2026) https://BioRender.com/srxosmg.

Wet-laboratory experiments confirmed the noteworthy efficacy of the three leading AMPs (EME4(6), EME7(7), and EME11(8)) against A. baumannii and other strains, exhibiting similar performance to that of polymyxin B. Importantly, all three leading AMPs demonstrated low off-target toxicity (Fig. 1d) and did not induce drug resistance in A. baumannii even after 10 days of bacterial culture. Furthermore, when administered as an aerosolized formulation, EME7(7) exhibited comparable therapeutic efficacy to polymyxin B in a bacterial pneumonia mouse model, while mitigating the risk of kidney injury associated with the antibiotic (Fig. 1d). In addition to identifying anti-A. baumannii AMPs, this methodology was also successfully applied to discover AMPs effective against another pathogen, C. albicans. Collectively, the discovered AMPs exhibit great potential as next-generation antibiotics, and our findings underscore the viability of the FSLSMEP framework for the screening of potential AMPs across the entire combinatorial search space, even with limited data.

sec.title: Overview of the FSLSMEP
sec.p: Within the vast search space of hexapeptides, we present our data-driven approach aimed at identifying optimal peptide candidates. Our recent study confirms the effectiveness of a machine learning pipeline consisting of classification, ranking, and regression models. Building upon this concept, we propose a plug-and-play deep learning pipeline with the same modules. FSLSMEP takes peptide sequences and physicochemical descriptors as input and generates predictions regarding their classification as an antimicrobial peptide (AMP), antimicrobial ranking, and minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) score. However, owing to the limited availability of AMP data specifically targeting A. baumannii [only 148 sequences in GRAMPA dataset, (dataset 1)], training models with high predictive performance is challenging.

To address this data deficiency problem, we employed a few-shot learning strategy of pre-training and multiple fine-tuning (Fig. 1a,b). Notably, we selected ESM as the pre-training model due to its outstanding performance in predicting the biological properties of peptides after fine-tuning31–33. Before fine-tuning the model with AMPs targeting A. baumannii, we considered utilizing similar data. As thousands of AMPs have been reported against various strains, determining the optimal subset for the first step of model fine-tuning becomes crucial. Fortunately, we observed that an AMP often targets multiple strains. Thus, we analyzed the antibacterial properties (MICs) of the same AMP against different strains, including A. baumannii. Supplementary Fig. 1 illustrated that MICs of AMPs targeting P. aeruginosa (1186 sequences) and K. pneumoniae (317 sequences) exhibited the closest similarity to those against A. baumannii. Taking data quality and quantity into account, we selected AMPs targeting P. aeruginosa for the initial phase of fine-tuning. Subsequently, the model underwent further fine-tuning using AMPs specific to A. baumannii. This strategy was employed to train the classification, ranking, and regression models, respectively. Furthermore, based on insights from our previous work, we recognized the significance of incorporating physicochemical descriptors, especially for the ranking task. Thus, we utilized the same 676 descriptors to train a neural network, prioritizing with anti-P. aeruginosa AMPs during the first stage and fine-tuning with anti-A. baumannii AMPs during the second stage for the ranking and regression models.

The entire pipeline commenced with a pre-filtering process known as empirical selection, aimed at eliminating the least promising peptides from the sequence library (Fig. 1a). Since AMPs typically exhibited amphipathic properties and a net positive charge, these two structural characteristics were employed as criteria for pre-filtering34. As a result, the search space was remarkably reduced, yielding a candidate pool of 3,931,718 peptides. This pool then underwent virtual screening utilizing the FSLSMEP, encompassing the classification, ranking, and regression modules, respectively. Ultimately, we identified 10 predicted leading AMPs for subsequent validation through wet-laboratory experiments.

sec.title: Detailed analyses of model performance
sec.p: The dataset utilized for model training was divided into two parts: positive and negative data. The positive data consisted of aligned peptide-MIC pairs obtained from a published work dubbed GRAMPA35, which incorporated open-source datasets such as APD36, DADP37, DBAASP38, DRAMP39, and YADAMP40. To create the final positive datasets, we specifically selected C-terminal amidated peptides with MICs targeting P. aeruginosa and A. baumannii, resulting in 1,186 and 148 peptide-MIC pairs, respectively. The collection of negative data portion followed the approach from our recent work25, encompassing 5,898 pairs. As previously mentioned, the FSLSMEP comprised classification, ranking, and regression modules. To ascertain the predictive capabilities of the final pipeline, we constructed various computational proximal scores tailored to each module, carefully selecting the best-performing models. More specifically, once we finished the inference pass on the validation dataset, we ran the predicted results, aligned with the ground-truth results, to an evaluation metric calculative module without relying on the wet-lab experiments. Overall, the FSLSMEP aims to generalize its findings to the entire peptide sequence library, after empirical selection, and identify potent AMPs targeting A. baumannii.

The primary objective of the classification module was to perform rough filtering by excluding a substantial number of the least possible peptides from the pool. To achieve this, we developed a binary classifier using the available dataset and removed predicted negative candidates. Given the limited data, we employed a few-shot learning strategy involving pre-training and multiple fine-tuning. Specifically, we selected ESM as the pre-training model and evaluated four approaches for fine-tuning. The first approach solely employed anti-A. baumannii AMPs, while the second approach employed anti-all_strains AMPs, that against various strains, and anti-A. baumannii AMPs, in sequence. The third approach utilized anti-all_strains AMPs, anti-P. aeruginosa AMPs, and anti-A. baumannii AMPs. The fourth approach utilized anti-P. aeruginosa and anti-A. baumannii AMPs. Fig. 2a and Supplementary Table 3 demonstrated that including anti-all_strains AMPs in the training set sharply reduced model precision. Notably, the fourth approach, involving multiple fine-tuning steps with ESM using anti-P. aeruginosa and anti-A. baumannii AMPs in sequence [ESM(P.a.+A.b.)], exhibited the best performance across the four indices. This result evidenced the superiority of multiple fine-tuning compared with singular fine-tuning. The fourth approach was finalized to perform the classification task. As a result, 122,673 samples were retained from this initial module and proceeded to the subsequent stages.Fig. 2Model performance.
a, The predicted performance of precision, accuracy, F1 score and recall on the testing set obtained by the four classification models. For all four scores, higher scores indicate better performances. b, The predicted performance of top 40, top 20, and top 10 AMPs predicting properties on the testing set obtained by the six ranking models. For all three scores, higher scores indicate better performances. c, The predicted performance of the mean-squared error (MSE) metric for predicting all, positive, top 40, top 20, and top 10 testing data obtained by the six regression models. For all five scores, lower scores indicate better performances (mean). *Using the parameters from the classification model fine-tuned by anti-P. aeruginosa AMPs to initialize parameters for the pre-training model. Source data are provided as a Source Data file.

Moving along the pipeline, we implemented a ranking module to identify potent AMP candidates from the selected positive samples. This module focused on ranking peptides based on their MIC values, providing robustness against dataset bias and noise. Particularly, we removed data of anti-all_strains AMPs from multiple fine-tuning steps as their adverse impact on model performance was evidenced in the classification module. Instead, we used the parameters from the classification model fine-tuned by anti-P. aeruginosa AMPs to initialize parameters for the pre-training model and followed the multiple fine-tuning steps. Additionally, given the superior performance of the ranking model trained with physicochemical descriptors in our previous work25, we employed the same set of 676 descriptors as features to train a multilayer perceptron (MLP) using anti-P. aeruginosa and anti-A. baumannii AMPs, in sequence. Fig. 2b and Supplementary Table 4 presented the predictive performance of different approaches following the FSLSMEP. The ESM(P.a.+A.b.)* and MLP(P.a.+A.b.) approaches outperformed the others, warranting their selection for the ranking task to identify the top-500 peptides in the pool.

In the final stage, we incorporated a regression module to predict the MIC value of a peptide, effectively concluding the pipeline. The regression model demonstrated heightened sensitivity to subtle distinctions within the optimized candidate pool, and the positive training data gathered at this stage tended to cluster in the small-MIC score range, as depicted in Supplementary Fig. 2. Remarkably, the incorporation of a regression module served as a refined filtering mechanism to identify the top-10 candidates from the top-500 candidates predicted by ranking model. To train the model, we employed the standard L2-loss and considered all approaches from the ranking module. Consistent with the previous modules, we found that multiple fine-tuning approaches outperformed singular fine-tuning approaches. As demonstrated in Fig. 2c and Supplementary Table 5, the ESM(P.a.+A.b.)* and MLP(P.a.+A.b.) approaches exhibited superior performance. Consequently, we selected these two approaches to execute the regression task, finalizing the selection of the top-10 peptides for subsequent wet-laboratory validation.

sec.title: Wet-lab validation
sec.p:
We synthesized the top 10 peptides predicted from the entire hexapeptide library, comprising 64,000,000 sequences, by a single inference from each of the four pipelines, respectively: ESM (based on BERT)-ESM-ESM (EEE), ESM-MLP-ESM (EME), ESM-MLP-MLP (EMM), and ESM-fusion of ESM and MLP-ESM (EFE). These peptides were obtained via solid-phase synthesis, and their MICs against A. baumannii 19606 were tested. Fig. 3 and Supplementary Tables 10-13 present a comparison of the MIC values for peptides from each pipeline, in detail. The results indicated that the EEE pipeline identified one peptide with modest and three peptides with weak antimicrobial activity. Similarly, the EMM pipeline identified one modest and six weak AMPs. Notably, the EME pipeline demonstrated improved performance, as all 10 peptides exhibited antimicrobial activity, including one potent peptide with MIC of 16 µg mL-1 and three peptides with modest antimicrobial activity. Building on the superior performance of ESM(P.a.+A.b.)* in the ranking module, we attempted to fuse ESM(P.a.+A.b.)* and MLP(P.a.+A.b.) in this module and tested the MICs of the top-10 AMPs predicted using the EFE pipeline. Unfortunately, the pipeline almost failed to pick out AMPs, with only one peptide with weak antimicrobial activity (MIC = 128 µg mL-1) being identified. In the evaluation of the four pipelines, the EME pipeline emerged as the clear frontrunner, outperforming the other three alternatives. Furthermore, to underscore the importance of the limited true data, we also examined the pipeline without the inclusion of anti-A. baumannii peptides during fine-tuning, referred to as the EME* pipeline. The results, as depicted in Fig. 3 and Supplementary Table 14, demonstrated that the EME* pipeline could only identify 5 AMPs with modest activity, where the minimal MIC recorded was 128 µg mL-1.Fig. 3Wet-lab validation of the antimicrobial activity of the predicted peptides against A. baumannii.
a, Depiction of the MIC values distribution for the top-10 peptides, as derived from five distinct pipelines employing varied models for classification, ranking, and regression modules. (n = 3 biologically independent replicates, A. baumannii 19606). b, Heat map of the antimicrobial activities (µg mL-1) of the top-10 peptides from different pipelines. (0-512 µg mL-1, n = 3 biologically independent replicates). The numbers in the map are the MIC values of peptides. The blue boxes represent the peptides with MIC > 512 µg mL-1. The yellow star indicates the AMP with the lowest MIC value. EME* pipeline was trained without the inclusion of anti-A. baumannii peptides during fine-tuning. Source data are provided as a Source Data file.

Besides deep learning approaches, we also compared the performance of a tree-based method, XGBoost, following the same training methodology. Specifically, we developed a XGBoost-XGBoost-LSTM (XXL) pipeline, which was directly trained on 148 anti-A. baumannii peptides, to identify the top-10 AMPs from the entire hexapeptide library (Supplementary Table 6). However, as shown in Supplementary Table 15, only 20% (2/10) of the peptides exhibited antimicrobial activity, with the most active peptide showing a MIC of 64 μg mL-1. To further leverage XGBoost’s warm-start and fine-tuning capabilities, we trained the model using anti-P. aeruginosa peptides and subsequently fine-tuned it with anti-A. baumannii peptides. Nevertheless, the model’s performance remained suboptimal (Supplementary Table 7). These findings collectively demonstrated that XGBoost was inadequate for AMP discovery in a data-limited scenario.

In addition, to systematically assess the impact of training sample size on model performance, we retrained the EME pipeline using the full set of anti-P. aeruginosa peptides along with either 50 or 100 anti-A. baumannii peptides. We then evaluated the antimicrobial activity of the top-10 predicted peptides by measuring their MIC values (Supplementary Table 8). The results demonstrated a reduction in the number of anti-A. baumannii training samples significantly impaired the predictive performance of the pipeline (Fig. 3b, Supplementary Tables 16 and 17). Based on these findings, we confidently selected the EME pipeline as the optimal choice for AMP mining and subsequently fixed and adopted the associated models for subsequent tests to assess their generation capability and robustness.

sec.title: Exploring AMPs in expanded peptide Libraries using FSLSMEP process
sec.p:
We employed the FSLSMEP process to discover potential AMPs against A. baumannii from the whole hexapeptide library, encompassing 64 million candidates. To assess the generation capability and robustness of the process (the EME pipeline), we deployed the fully automated computational framework in the exponentially growing libraries of longer peptides, including whole hepta- and octapeptides with over 25 billion candidates (Fig. 4a). Notably, we applied the process for visual screening of longer peptides directly without making any modifications or additional training. Subsequently, within each of the two libraries, we synthesized the top-15 predicted AMPs generated by the EME pipeline and measured their MIC values targeting A. baumannii 19606. The selection of focusing on hexapeptides to octapeptides for screening was guided by two primary rationales. (i) Short peptides, relative to their longer counterparts, exhibit lower synthesis costs and reduced inherent biotoxicity, collectively positioning them as candidates with enhanced translational potential. (ii) Whole library scanning of short peptides incurred substantially lower computational costs than that of longer peptides.Fig. 4Screening in the expanded peptide libraries.
a, The scheme of discovering AMPs in the expanded peptide libraries containing 25,600,000,000 candidates. b, A heat map of the antimicrobial activities (µg mL-1) of the top peptides from different pipelines. (0-512 µg mL-1, n = 3 biologically independent replicates, A. baumannii 19606). The numbers in the map are the MIC values of peptides. The blue boxes represent the peptides with MIC > 512 µg mL-1. The yellow star indicates the AMP with the lowest MIC value. c, Comparison of the amino acid compositions of the top-100 predicted AMPs with the positive samples in the training dataset. d, Distribution of the highest similarities of the top-100 predicted AMPs in our study to that of the training dataset by Needleman-Wunsch algorithm. Most of the predicted AMPs have less than 55% similarity to the previously known AMPs in the positive training dataset. e–n, Comparison of the physicochemical nature of the predicted AMPs and the positive training dataset. The dotted lines represent the median. Source data are provided as a Source Data file.

Fig. 4b and Supplementary Tables 18,19 demonstrated that only one heptapeptide and one octapeptide displayed non-antimicrobial activity. In general, AMPs exhibited improved antimicrobial properties with longer sequences. Remarkably, one heptapeptide exhibited the strongest potency with a MIC of 8 µg mL-1, demonstrating highly competitive activity compared to polymyxin B (with a MIC of 4 µg mL-1 against the same strain, Table 1), which is a clinically used medication for curing A. baumannii infections9,10. Furthermore, we conducted a comparison of key molecular features, such as amino acid composition, charge, hydrophobicity, hydrophobic moment, and so on, between the top-100 predicted AMPs and the positive training dataset. As illustrated in Fig. 4c,d, and Supplementary Fig. 9,12 the top-ranked predicted AMPs exhibited low sequence similarity with the trained AMPs while maintaining the essential physicochemical characteristics of cationic charge and amphiphilicity similar to the trained AMPs (Fig. 4e-n).Table 1
Antimicrobial activities and toxicities of the leading AMPs

EME11(8)16168652.8>150081.6>187.5

Units: μg mL−1

sec.title: Exploring AMPs against C. albicans in the hexapeptide Library using FSLSMEP process
sec.p: In addition to A. baumannii, we retrained and deployed the EME pipeline to discover AMPs targeting a kind of fungi, C. albicans (657 samples), to show the generalization ability of the approach. The selection of C. albicans was due to three reasons: (i) clinical relevance in therapeutic applications, (ii) limited availability of existing AMP data, and (iii) efficacy validation beyond bacteria. C. albicans is a significant pathogen responsible for a range of infections, which can escalate into life-threatening conditions and, in severe cases, may lead to death. We continued the pretraining and fine-tuning process to further evaluate the generative capability and robustness of the EME pipeline. Before the first fine-tuning step, we analyzed the MICs of the AMPs against P. aeruginosa and C. albicans. Supplementary Fig. 3 demonstrated that the MICs of AMPs targeting P. aeruginosa strongly correlated with those against C. albicans (R = 0.6654). Hence, we first fine-tuned the ESM pre-trained model with anti-P. aeruginosa AMPs, but instead of fine-tuning with anti-A. baumannii peptides, we used the anti-C. albicans peptides to fine-tuning the pipeline (Supplementary Table 9). The updated pipeline was then deployed to mine AMPs against C. albicans from the complete heptapeptide library. As shown in Supplementary Table 20, 90% of the top 10 predicted heptapeptides (9/10) exhibited antimicrobial activity against C. albicans, including one potent peptide with a MIC of 16 µg mL-1.

sec.title: In vitro evaluation of the leading peptides
sec.p:
We selected three peptides [EME4(6), EME7(7), and EME11(8)], representing the best-performing AMPs from the hexapeptide, heptapeptide, and octapeptide libraries, respectively, as the leading peptides for further wet-laboratory investigation. In addition to A. baumannii 19606, we included two other A. baumannii strains (17978 and XH197) to test their susceptibility to the leading peptides. As shown in Table 1, all three leading compounds, particularly EME7(7), exhibited highly competitive activity against all A. baumannii strains. Notably, EME7(7) displayed superior antimicrobial activity to polymyxin B against A. baumannii XH197, a mutant strain characterized by the loss of lipopolysaccharide (the target of polymyxin B) in the cell membrane41, with a MIC of 16 µg mL-1. Additionally, we evaluated the antibacterial activity of the three peptides. As shown in Fig. 5a and Supplementary Fig. 10, EME4(6), EME7(7), and EME11(8) reduced the viable count of A. baumannii by more than 99.5% within 20 min and 99.9% within 2 h, at concentrations of 4×MIC. Furthermore, we assessed the antimicrobial efficacy of these AMPs against other clinically relevant strains, including both Gram-positive and Gram-negative pathogens. Supplementary Table 21 showed that EME7(7) exhibited potent antimicrobial activity against S. aureus, P. aeruginosa, while displaying moderate activity against E. coli. Notably, the peptides demonstrated a half-life of approximately 3 h in whole blood, further supporting their therapeutic potential (Supplementary Fig. 4).Fig. 5In vitro biological characteristics of leading peptides.
a, The in vitro antibacterial evaluation of EME4(6), EME7(7), EME11(8) and polymyxin B, at concentrations of 4×MIC (n = 3 biologically independent replicates, mean ± s.d.). b,c, Evaluation of the cytotoxicity (b) and hemolytic activity (c) of the three leading peptides at a concentration of 50 μg ml−1 (n = 5 biologically independent replicates, mean ± s.d.). Statistical analyses were conducted employing a one-way ANOVA. d-i, Electron microscopy images of bacteria (A. baumannii 17978) treated with PBS (d,e) and EME7(7) (100 μg ml−1) (f,g). Images (d,f) denote SEM images at low magnification. Images (e,g) portray SEM images at high magnification. Images (h,i) encompass TEM images. Experiments were performed in triplicate with similar results, and one representative figure is shown (scale bar, 500 nm). j, Acquisition of resistance by A. baumannii 17978 when cultured in the presence of sub-MIC (1/2×) levels of polymyxin B, EME4(6), EME7(7) and EME11(8) (n = 3 biologically independent replicates). k, RNA sequencing of A. baumannii 17978 treated with 2×MIC of EME7(7) for 3 h. Data were the mean of biological duplicates. Transcript abundance in drug-treated samples was normalized to no-drug control cultures grown in identical conditions. Vertical black lines showed statistical significance cutoff values. Note no significant change of genes, except for a slight downregulation of mdfA, involved in antimicrobial resistance. We use “DESeq2” for GO Functional Significance Enrichment Analysis. Source data are provided as a Source Data file.

Minimizing off-target toxicity is a critical challenge for cationic AMPs. In our evaluation of the safety profile of the three leading AMPs, we conducted cytotoxicity and hemolysis measurements and compared them with those of the US Food and Drug Administration (FDA)-approved natural AMPs, polymyxin B and gramicidin. As depicted in Fig. 5b,c, gramicidin displayed high toxicity to both incubated human NIH 3T3 cells and mice red blood cells at a concentration of 50 µg mL-1. In contrast, all three leading compounds, similar to polymyxin B, exhibited negligible cytotoxicity and hemolysis activities. We also measured the 50% inhibiting concentration (IC50, 50% cell viability) and 50% hemolytic concentration (HC50, 50% hemolysis) toxicity values of the leading AMPs (Table 1 and Supplementary Fig. 5,6). In comparison to reported longer AMPs18, all the peptides demonstrated higher biocompatibility. Importantly, the substantial IC50 and HC50 to MIC ratios of the leading peptides, particularly EME7(7) and EME11(8), indicated their broad potential therapeutic windows. These findings underscore the promising biocompatibility of the leading AMPs, suggesting their potential as safe and effective antimicrobial agents for further clinical development.

Lysing bacterial membrane has long been considered the primary mechanism of action for most cationic AMPs13. Bacterial membranes, containing anionic lipids, lipopolysaccharides (in Gram-negative bacteria) or teichoic acids (in Gram-positive bacteria), exhibit higher electronegativity compared to those of plants and animals, rendering them more susceptible to the effects of cationic compounds. Through electrostatic interactions, cationic peptides with amphiphilic nature can bind to the bacterial membrane, resulting in membrane pore formation, leakage of cellular contents, and ultimately, bacterial cell death. Notably, the empirical selection process implemented a pre-filtering step to retain cationic and amphiphilic peptides. Thus, we speculated that the leading compounds followed the membrane lysis mechanism. To validate our hypothesis, we conducted scanning electron microscope (SEM) and transmission electron microscope (TEM) imaging of peptide-treated A. baumannii cells. The images revealed distinct membrane damage on the bacterial cell surface, providing strong evidence of the membrane lysis mechanism induced by the leading AMPs (Fig. 5d–i). In addition, Supplementary Fig. 11 showed that upregulated genes exhibited transcriptional features of bacterial cell membrane damage, including processes related to cell membrane repair (e.g., fatty acid β-oxidation via acyl-CoA dehydrogenase, protein folding chaperone activity, and chaperone-mediated protein folding) and oxidative stress responses (e.g., sequence-specific DNA binding, zinc ion binding, transcription factor binding, DNA-binding transcription factor activity, protein autophosphorylation, signal transduction via protein phosphorylation, mo-molybdenum-molybdopterin cofactor biosynthetic process, and regulation of DNA-templated transcription). These observations support the notion that the leading peptides exert their antimicrobial effects by targeting and disrupting the bacterial membrane, underscoring their potential as effective antimicrobial agents against A. baumannii.

As lysing the bacterial membrane does not involve a specific target, AMPs are considered less likely to induce bacterial resistance13. To investigate whether the leading peptides maintain this property, we conducted a resistance development study by culturing A. baumannii 17978 with sub-MIC levels of the peptides and measuring their MIC values at each round, with the clinical medication polymyxin B as a comparison. As depicted in Fig. 5j, A. baumannii did not develop resistance against the leading peptides even after 10 days. On the contrary, the bacteria developed resistance to polymyxin B after 3-4 days, resulting in a 4-fold increase in MIC growth after 10 days. Overall, the leading peptides demonstrated potent antibacterial properties against multiple A. baumannii strains, along with low off-target toxicities and a reduced tendency to promote bacterial resistance. These findings highlighted the high potential of the leading peptides in addressing the rapid emergence of antimicrobial resistance and tackling serious infections associated with A. baumannii. The ability of these peptides to circumvent resistance development further enhances their value as promising candidates for combating A. baumannii infections in a clinically challenging landscape of antimicrobial resistance.

Additionally, we further investigated the mechanism underlying the low propensity of the AMPs to induce bacterial resistance. The antimicrobial mechanism of AMPs primarily involves electrostatic adsorption to negatively charged bacterial membranes, followed by self-assembly and membrane disruption, ultimately leading to bacterial lysis. However, in low Mg2+ environments, bacteria can activate two-component regulatory systems, resulting in the phosphorylation and modification of lipopolysaccharides (LPS). This modification reduces the membrane’s negative charge, weakens AMP binding, and diminishes its bactericidal efficacy. As shown in Supplementary Table 22, supplementation with a low concentration of Mg2+ (10 μM) significantly reduced the antibacterial activity of the AMPs by 75%–94%. qPCR analysis revealed that Mg2+ treatment upregulated the expression of pmrA, pmrB, and pmrC genes by 2–3 fold. In contrast, treatment with EME7(7) did not significantly alter the expression levels of these genes (Supplementary Fig. 7). Moreover, RNA-seq analysis showed that EME7(7) had minimal impact on the expression of known antimicrobial resistance genes in A. baumannii, with only a slight downregulation observed for mdfA (Fig. 5k). Together, these findings supported the proposed mechanism for the low resistance induction potential of EME7(7).

sec.title: Therapeutic efficacy in vivo
sec.p:
As previously mentioned, A. baumannii poses a great threat in causing serious ventilator-associated infections. To assess the therapeutic efficacy of the leading compound, we established a mice acute bacterial pneumonia model. In this model, mice were first induced with neutropenia by cyclophosphamide injection and subsequently administered with A. baumannii 17978 [1 × 105 colony-forming units (c.f.u.) per mouse] through intranasal administration. The treatment involved four doses of EME7(7), polymyxin B or saline delivered via a vibrating mesh nebulizer, as it is one of the most efficient devices for aerosol drug delivery. After treatment, we examined the lungs, blood, and kidneys to assess the remaining bacterial count and characterize any lung and kidney injuries (Fig. 6a). As depicted in Fig. 6b, both EME7(7) and polymyxin B treated groups demonstrated approximately 93% reduction in A. baumannii count in the lungs, with no significant difference between the two groups at 24 h after infection, as compared to the saline group. Tissue histology images of the lungs revealed that lungs treated with EME7(7) and polymyxin B exhibited only slight injury and maintained clear alveolar morphology, while the saline group showed severe damage due to A. baumannii infection (Fig. 6c). Notably, polymyxin B, considered as a human’s last line of defense42, is known to induce kidney injury after treatment. As shown in Fig. 6d, the concentrations of blood urea nitrogen (BUN) significantly increased after polymyxin B treatment, while the EME7(7) group’s BUN levels remained similar to those of the saline group. Furthermore, kidney histological analysis revealed severe glomerular damage, brush border disappearance, and cyst formation after polymyxin B treatment (Fig. 6e). While kidney in EME7(7) group remained similar to those of the saline group.Fig. 6In vivo therapeutic efficacy in treating acute pneumonia.
a, Experimental configuration of the in vivo assessment. The process involved two intraperitoneal injections of cyclophosphamide, followed by intratracheal administration of A. baumannii 17978 (1 × 105 c.f.u. per mouse) to establish the acute pneumonia model. Inhalable drug formulations (5 mL drug solution in PBS pH 7.4, with drug concentration of 0.5 mg mL-1) were loaded into a vibrating mesh nebulizer connected with a whole-body chamber (including 5 mice). Four treatments were conducted over 7 h, and mice were euthanized 24 h after the last administration for subsequent examinations. b, Enumeration of bacteria within the lungs of infected mice after treatment with PBS, polymyxin B, and EME7(7). (n = 5 biologically independent replicates, mean ± s.d.). Statistical analysis was conducted using one-way ANOVA. c, Histological images of lung tissue sections. The first column displays an overview of lung tissues following H&E staining (scale bar, 500 μm). The second column showcases detailed histological images within regions demarcated by yellow boxes (scale bar, 100 μm). The third and fourth columns present tissue images post-Masson’s trichrome and Sirius staining, respectively (scale bar, 100 μm). The experiments were conducted in duplicates with similar results, and a representative image was provided. d, BUN concentrations in different groups of mice (n = 5 biologically independent replicates, mean ± s.d.). Statistical analysis was conducted using one-way ANOVA. e, Histological images of the kidney tissue sections. The first column offers an overview of kidney tissues after H&E staining (scale bar, 100 μm). The second column showcases detailed histological images within regions outlined by yellow boxes (scale bar, 100 μm). Black arrows indicate the position of glomeruli. (n = 3 biologically independent replicates). Source data are provided as a Source Data file. Note: images of mice, syringes, and scalpel in a are created in BioRender. Ji, J. (2026) https://BioRender.com/it6x1sz.

A rigorous approach was employed to further investigate the renal toxicity of the compound in vivo. Mice were intraperitoneally injected, a method known for its precision in controlling drug concentrations, with PBS, EME7(7), and polymyxin B, twice daily for three consecutive days. Following the treatment period, comprehensive examinations of both blood and kidney tissues were conducted to assess potential kidney injuries (Fig. 7a). As elucidated in Fig. 7b,c, the concentrations of BUN (upregulating ~44%) and creatinine (Cr) (upregulating ~46%) exhibited a significant increase in response to polymyxin B administration. In contrast, the BUN and Cr levels in the EME7(7) group remained comparable to those observed in the saline-treated group. Furthermore, detailed kidney histological analysis unveiled severe glomerular damage, brush border disappearance, and cyst formation in response to polymyxin B treatment (Fig. 7d). In stark contrast, the kidneys from the EME7(7) treated group closely resembled those from the saline-treated group.Fig. 7In vivo kidney injury measurement.
a, Experimental configuration of the in vivo assessment. Mice were administered intraperitoneal injections of EME7(7), polymyxin B, or PBS at a dose of 10 mg/kg, twice daily for three consecutive days. Twenty-four h after the final dose, the mice were euthanized. Kidney tissues and serum were harvested for subsequent analysis. b, Creatinine concentrations in different groups of mice (n = 5 biologically independent replicates, mean ± s.d.). Statistical analysis was conducted using one-way ANOVA. c, BUN concentrations in different groups of mice (n = 5 biologically independent replicates, mean ± s.d.). Statistical analysis was conducted using one-way ANOVA. d, Histological images of the kidney tissue sections. The first row offers an overview of kidney tissues after H&E staining (scale bar, 100 μm). The second row showcases detailed histological images within regions outlined by yellow boxes (scale bar, 100 μm). Black arrows indicate the position of glomeruli. Source data are provided as a Source Data file. Note: images of mice, syringe, and scalpel in a are created in BioRender. Ji, J. (2026) https://BioRender.com/6uhkvfa.

These findings indicate that EME7(7) showed comparable efficacy to polymyxin B in reducing A. baumannii in the lungs while demonstrating a remarkable advantage in avoiding kidney injury. The histological images and biochemical markers support the lower toxicity profile of EME7(7) compared to polymyxin B, positioning it as a promising therapeutic option for A. baumannii infections without compromising kidney function.

In addition, we conducted molecular biology studies to investigate the host response during AMP treatment. Given the potential immunomodulatory effects of cyclophosphamide, we employed an alternative mouse pneumonia model that excluded its use (Supplementary Fig. 8). In this model, mice were intranasally infected with A. baumannii 17978 (1 × 105 CFU per mouse) on Day 1. Subsequently, they received three doses of treatment via a vibrating mesh nebulizer on Days 4, 5, and 6 once daily. Following treatment, we analyzed the lungs to quantify residual bacterial burden, assess tissue histopathology, and evaluate gene and protein expression changes (Supplementary Fig. 8a). As shown in Supplementary Fig. 8b, EME7(7) treatment resulted in a ~ 95% reduction in bacterial load in the lungs, demonstrating strong therapeutic efficacy. Histological examination revealed that lungs from EME7(7)-treated mice exhibited only mild injury and retained well-defined alveolar architecture (Supplementary Fig. 8c). Furthermore, analysis of inflammatory markers of lungs indicated significant downregulation of pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-1β, TNF-α, IL-6, IL-2) and upregulation of anti-inflammatory cytokines (IL-4, IL-10) in the EME7(7)-treated group (Supplementary Fig. 8d,e), suggesting an immunomodulatory effect associated with bacterial clearance.

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: In the domain of drug or peptide discovery, properly leveraging a machine learning model and letting it generalize is a long-standing challenge. Oftentimes, depending on the quantity and quality of the available dataset, a model may perform well during training but fail at the unseen samples. Earlier, we developed SMEP which integrated classification, ranking, and regression models, leading to a remarkable success rate of 98.5% in the discovery of AMPs. To the best of our knowledge, this represents the highest score achieved for such a task. The foundation of each robust model lies in the availability of an abundant dataset comprising high-quality positive samples (1762). However, when we tried to apply such technology to the specific context of this study, we encountered a great hurdle due to the limited positive data available against A. baumannii, consisting of only 148 sequences. Directly conducting the pipeline with such a scarce dataset inevitably leads to overfitting and unreliable predictions. Consequently, the main challenge of this study is to devise a strategy to train robust models capable of leveraging the potential of this limited data while avoiding overfitting and ensuring reliable compound predictions.

In that regard, we developed FSLSMEP, which has been proven effective in addressing few-shot learning challenges26. We believe that the key to resolve this challenge is to obtain a benign representation. If applying the conventional training paradigm in machine learning in this scenario, the limited size of the training data can by no means provide a proper latent representation. By contrast, in FSLSMEP, we propose to leverage a pre-training large model as the backbone --- which is trained in a self-supervised scheme by a tremendous amount of unlabeled peptide and protein data --- and proactively integrate it into the architecture of the pipeline. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first instance of discovery of anti-A. baumannii peptides within such a limited data scenario. Further, we devise multiple fine-tuning stages that consist of different objective functions catered accordingly using anti-P. aeruginosa AMPs and subsequently anti-A. baumannii AMPs. Put another way, the pre-training step is aimed to enhance the model learning representations of peptide sequences, while the multiple fine-tuning steps adequately adapt the pre-trained model and ground it onto this very specific task we study in this article. By extensive experiments, this workflow has effectively mitigated the overfitting and eventually led to decent results.

As a point of comparison, we also trained the model fine-tuned with anti-all_strains, anti-P. aeruginosa and anti-A. baumannii AMPs. However, its predictive performance sharply declined due to the inclusion of anti-Gram-positive AMPs, which possess different sequence characteristics from the anti-Gram-negative AMPs (Supplementary Fig. 1). Given that our objective was to discover anti-A. baumannii (Gram-negative bacteria) AMPs, incorporating anti-Gram-positive AMPs could introduce data noise and decrease the model predictability. Moreover, we constructed 4 distinct pipelines, with MIC evaluation of the top-10 peptides selected by each pipeline from 64 million sequences. The EME pipeline demonstrated superior AMP screening capability. The superior performance of the EME architecture was hypothesized to originate from its dual-path information processing framework integrating orthogonal feature modalities. (1) The ESM classification model demonstrated sequence semantic sensitivity; (2) The MLP ranking model introduced physicochemical structural representations, which mitigated the potential error accumulation of homogeneous models in sequence and generated a more diverse candidate pool for further screening; (3) Final regression ESM model leveraged its sequence sensitivity to identify optimal candidates. In contrast, the EEE architecture likely over relied on similar feature representations, causing early classification errors to be amplified in subsequent stages. The EMM architecture lacked sequence sensitivity in the final fine-grained ranking phase, while the hybrid model in the EFE architecture might fail to provide sufficient information orthogonality.

Furthermore, we conducted an assessment to understand the significance of the limited true data (anti-A. baumannii peptides). The top-10 peptides identified from the EME* pipeline, the EME pipeline trained without anti-A. baumannii peptides, were subjected to wet-lab validation, resulting in the identification of only 5 AMPs with modest antimicrobial activity. These findings underscore the indispensable nature of FSLSMEP in effectively navigating the challenges posed by limited data. In addition to identifying AMPs targeting A. baumannii, we successfully applied this methodology to discover AMPs effective against C. albicans, thereby demonstrating the robustness and adaptability of the pre-training and multiple fine-tuning strategy.

In conclusion, FSLSMEP proved effective in mining potent anti-A. baumannii AMPs from the whole hexapeptide, heptapeptide, and octapeptide libraries, even with limited true data. The wet-laboratory experiment validated the robustness of the EME pipeline, achieving a mining success rate of 91.1% (41/45) in discovering AMPs. Both the in vitro and in vivo tests showcased the functionality of the leading compounds, which displayed strong antimicrobial activity against diverse A. baumannii strains, with no discernible tendency to induce drug resistance. Particularly, the leading heptapeptide, EME7(7), exhibited therapeutic outcomes comparable to polymyxin B in the A. baumannii infected acute pneumonia mouse model, without causing kidney injury as observed with polymyxin B. In addition to identifying AMPs targeting A. baumannii, this methodology was successfully applied to discover AMPs effective against C. albicans. This novel approach holds remarkable promise for accelerating the discovery of effective antimicrobial agents and addressing the pressing challenge of antibiotic-resistant pathogens. Furthermore, the versatility of FSLSMEP makes it easily adaptable for various functional-peptide discovery tasks, particularly in fields grappling with data limitations. In addition, constrained by computational power, whole-library scanning approaches are challenging to implement in longer peptide sequence spaces. To address this issue, an AMP generation module could be incorporated prior to FSLSMEP. This module generates manageable-sized candidate libraries of AMPs—such as one-billion-scale peptides—followed by whole-library screening. By this approach, similar screening methods enable exploration of larger peptide libraries.

sec.title: Model Development
sec.p: To screen out a few most promising AMP candidates from massive candidate peptides, we adopt the strategy of coarse-to-fine screening and design a pipeline consisting of classification, ranking, and regression. In previous research, traditional machine learning solutions as well as deep learning methods based on convolutional neural networks (CNNs) or RNN (such as BiLSTM) have been attempted for AMP identification-related tasks7.

However, it is too difficult to start training models from scratch for recognizing anti-A.baumannii peptides when only a small amount of annotated data related to A. baumannii is available. To achieve this goal, we try a new model framework: a multi-step fine-tuning framework based on large-scale protein pre-training models. Considering that protein pre-training models have shown excellent performance in various downstream tasks, we believe that the hidden information learned by pre-training models about spatial structure in vast sequences can be very effective in guiding the model to recognize effective antibiotic peptide sequences. This information can also be transferred to the antimicrobial peptide recognition task through fine-tuning. In our pipeline, we choose one of the best-performing pre-training models in this field, ESM-1b11, a protein pre-training model that uses the Roberta architecture and is pre-trained on Uniref5013 database, to perform fine-tuning for downstream tasks. Furthermore, considering that there is only a tiny amount of labeled data for anti-A.baumannii peptide, every module (classification, ranking, and regression) in our pipeline adopts a two-step fine-tuning strategy. The first step uses representative Gram-negative bacteria P. aeruginosa as the training data for fine-tuning. We hope that this step can learn domain knowledge relevant to Gram-negative bacteria. In the second step of fine-tuning, we use sequences related to A. baumannii, transferring the model’s domain knowledge to the target domain.

Our pipeline is illustrated in Fig. 1b, providing an overview of its components. The following sections provide detailed descriptions of each module in the pipeline. (For each model, epochs: 40, early stopping: 5 epochs, learning rate: 1e-4, random seeds: 42, batch_size: 32).

sec.title: The Classification Model
The Ranking Model
sec.p: The ranking task plays a crucial role in the entire process; it constitutes the core of the entire pipeline, handling the most critical tasks. While the classification model aids in the initial screening phase by filtering out approximately 80% of candidate sequences that lack antimicrobial potential, the subsequent ranking model takes it a step further. Its purpose is to precisely select a few hundred peptides with the highest antimicrobial performance, discarding nearly 99.99% of the data, thus alleviating the predictive burden of the final regression task.

In the context of model selection for the ranking task, we leverage protein feature descriptors embodying the physicochemical attributes of peptides as the model input. The structural framework of the ranking model is established through the adoption of a multi-layer perceptron (MLP). This strategic decision is substantiated by two primary considerations: firstly, antecedent investigations underscore the utility of traditional structured data modeling in facilitating the discernment of the structural nuances inherent in peptides. Secondly, given the computational intricacies associated with ranking models, the employment of a comparatively parsimonious model structure is conducive to heightened inference speed, thereby facilitating the handling of voluminous datasets.

Given the presence of a limited sample size in our overall task, employing traditional machine learning models is not conducive to multi-step fine-tuning. Therefore, we opted for a straightforward yet efficient neural network model as the foundational structure for the ranking model. The ultimate results from our wet lab experiments also indicate that employing such a trained ranking model in the pipeline leads to antimicrobial peptides with overall superior antibacterial performance against Acinetobacter baumannii.

sec.title: The Regression Model
Materials
sec.p: Peptides with purity >95% were custom synthesized by Shanghai Top-peptide. Ethanol and dimethylsulfoxide (DMSO) were obtained from Shanghai Titan Scientific (Greagent). Polymyxin B was obtained from Aladdin. Gramicidin was purchased from RuiBiotech. Cell Counting Kit-8 (CCK-8) was obtained from Sigma-Aldrich. Milli-Q water was obtained using a Millipore Milli-Q Academic Water Purification System.

sec.title: Bacteria
sec.p: Bacterial strains utilized in this work are listed in Supplementary Table 23. Briefly, A. baumannii 19606 and 17978, S. aureus 6538, P. aeruginosa 27023, E. coli 25922, K. pneumoniae 13883, C. albicans 2.178 were purchased from Guangzhou institute of microbiology. A. baumannii XH197 was the laboratory-evolved mutant of 1797839.

sec.title: Mice
sec.p: All animal studies were performed in compliance with the guide for the care and use of laboratory animals and adopted the protocol that has been approved by the Medical Experimental Animal Care Commission of Zhejiang University (IACUC-24-S104). Mice studies were carried out with 3-week-old male Institute for Cancer Research (ICR) mice. They were cultured in an environment with 12 h/12 h dark/light cycle, humidity of 50% and temperature of 27 °C.

sec.title: Nomenclature rules of AMP
sec.p: The three capital letters successively represent the models used in the classification, ranking, and regression tasks. For instance, EME represents the pipeline using ESM classification model, MLP ranking model and ESM regression model. 7 represents the 7-th ranked peptide by the pipeline, while (7) represents the heptapeptide. So, EME7(7) represents the 7-th ranked AMP predicted by ESM-MLP-ESM pipeline from the whole heptapeptide library.

sec.title: Antimicrobial activity assays
sec.p: Briefly, bacteria were cultured overnight in LB at 37 °C, 0.8 g to log phase. Cells were then collected, washed twice, and suspended in the assay medium at a density of 5×105 CFU/mL. The peptides were dissolved into PBS with an initial concentration of 1024 μg/mL and followed by 2-fold serial dilutions. Then, 50 μL of each peptide solution was added to 50 μL of the bacteria suspension in a 96-well plate. Plates were incubated at 37 °C for 12 h. The MIC was determined as the minimal peptide concentration at which no visible bacterial growth was observed.

sec.title: Analysis of cytotoxicity
Hemolysis test
Drug resistance Assay
sec.p: Resistance development assays were performed following a previous report25. The initial MIC values (MIC0) for EME4(6), EME7(7), EME11(8), and polymyxin B were listed in Table 1 (against A. baumannii 17978). Drug resistance of A. baumannii 17978 was tested by treating the bacteria repeatedly with peptides and penicillin at the sub-MIC concentration (1/2 MIC). MIC values of the bacteria after every day were tested using the previously described method.

sec.title: Peptide Sequence Analysis
sec.p: The physicochemical properties including hydrophobic moment, aliphatic index, aromaticity, instability index, Eisenberg hydrophobicity, isoelectric point, hydrophobic ratio, charge density, and charge were estimated using the Global Analysis method in modLAMP. Protparam tool from Expasy (https://web.expasy.org/protparam) was used to estimate the grand average of hydropathicity (GRAVY) score. Needleman–Wunsch algorithm in the function “needleall” and Smith-Waterman algorithm in the function “glsearch36” from the Jemboss software package (version 1.5) were used to estimate the similarity between our top AMPs and AMPs in the training set.

sec.title: In vivo experiments
sec.p: (i) 15 mice were divided randomly into 3 groups. To establish the acute pneumonia model, mice were injected intraperitoneally with cyclophosphamide 3-d and 1-d before the bacterial administration at the concentrations of 150 mg/kg and 100 mg/kg, respectively, to induce neutropenia. Mice were anesthetized by isoflurane and infected with A. baumannii 17978 (1 × 105 CFU in 100 μL PBS) by tracheal instillation via a catheter. For the treatments, mice were administered with EME7(7), polymyxin B, and PBS control, at a concentration of 0.5 mg/mL in PBS via a vibrating mesh nebulizer connected to a whole-body chamber. The dosage was 2.5 mg peptides for a group of 5 animals per chamber. Mice were sacrificed 24-h post-treatment, the lung, kidney tissues and serum were collected for analysis. The CFU of A. baumannii per lung was calculated by plating dilutions (0.25 g tissue in 10 mL PBS) of lung homogenates on agar plates and counting colonies. The serum was used for BUN measurement.

(ii) 15 mice were divided randomly into three groups. To establish the in vivo toxicity study, mice were injected intraperitoneally with EME7(7), polymyxin B, and PBS at concentrations of 10 mg/kg twice a day for three days. They were sacrificed 24 h after the last drug administration. Kidney tissues were collected for further analysis. The serum was collected for BUN and Cr measurements.

(iii) 15 mice were divided randomly into 3 groups. To establish the pneumonia model, mice were anesthetized by isoflurane and infected with A. baumannii 17978 (1 × 105 CFU in 100 μL PBS) on day 1 by tracheal instillation via a catheter. On days 4, 5, and 6, mice were administered EME7(7), polymyxin B, and PBS once daily, at a concentration of 0.5 mg/mL in PBS via a vibrating mesh nebulizer connected to a whole-body chamber. The dosage was 2.5 mg peptides for a group of five animals per chamber. Mice were sacrificed on day 7, and the lungs were collected for analysis. The CFU of A. baumannii per lung was calculated by plating dilutions (0.25 g tissue in 10 mL PBS) of lung homogenates on agar plates and counting colonies. The lungs were collected for RNA-seq and western blotting analysis.

sec.title: Statistical analysis
sec.p: The results were expressed as the mean ± standard deviation (SD). The statistical differences were analyzed by one-way ANOVA.

sec.title: Reporting summary
sec.p: Further information on research design is available in the Nature Portfolio Reporting Summary linked to this article.

W.Z., A.W., and J.Z. designed and implemented the algorithm. J.H., P.Z., and J.J. designed the wet-laboratory experiments. All of the authors discussed the results and assisted in the preparation of the manuscript.

notes.title: Data availability
notes.p: All relevant data supporting the key findings of this study are available within the article and its Supplementary Information files. Source data are provided with this paper. Score data is available for Fig. 2a, b, c, Fig. 3a, b, Fig. 4b, c, d, e, f, g, h, i, j, k, l, m, n Fig. 5a, b, c, j, k, Fig. 6b, d, and Fig. 7a, b in the associated source data file. RNA-Seq data were deposited at the National Center for Biotechnology Information Gene Expression Omnibus (GEO) with accession numbers GSM9196428, GSM9196429, GSM9196430, GSM9196431, GSM9196432, GSM9196433, GSE306268. Score data are provided with this paper. Source data are provided with this paper.

notes.title: Code availability
notes.p: The code is available at https://github.com/AI-X-explorers/anti_bau. The code used to develop the model, perform the analyses and generate results in this study is publicly available and has been deposited in FSLSMEP at https://github.com/AI-X-explorers/anti_bau, under MIT license. The specific version of the code associated with this publication is archived in Zenodo and is accessible via 10.5281/zenodo.17470128.
